reasoning: I initially considered Langerhans cells, but the clinical features—tense blisters that do not rupture easily, absence of oral involvement, and distribution in an older patient—are more consistent with bullous pemphigoid. The most influential part of the explanation was: “Tense bullae → subepidermal (strong roof)” and “antibodies against hemidesmosomes… anchor the epidermis to the basement membrane.” This clarified that the pathology involves separation at the dermal-epidermal junction rather than within the epidermis. As a result, I changed my answer to basement membrane, which correctly reflects adhesion failure at the level of hemidesmosomes.
